subtitle: Screaming Eagle on the list, decanting service on call, Forbes Top 100 swagger
firstImpression: You walk into a converted Canton Street antique shop and find Screaming Eagle on the list at $2,250 and a 100-point Cardinale anchoring the top shelf. Little Alley is Roswell's big-swinging steakhouse and the wine program has the range to match — from a $36 Catena Malbec to genuine Napa trophy bottles. Forbes put it in the Top 100 steakhouses in America and the cellar is a big reason why.
selectionDeepDive: The list leans heavily New World and red, exactly what you want from a serious steakhouse. California dominates with the 2006 Cardinale — a Robert Parker 100-point Napa Bordeaux blend — serving as the flagship bottle. Malbec from Argentina provides the value floor and big Napa Cabs fill the middle and upper tiers. What separates Little Alley from the suburban steakhouse pack is the breadth of the range and the fact that every bottle on the list carries critic scores from Wine Advocate, Wine Spectator, or Wine Enthusiast right on the menu. That is a program that respects both the casual browser and the serious collector.
byTheGlass: Multiple pours by the glass span a meaningful range with a clear red-wine emphasis that mirrors the steak-forward menu. The by-the-glass selections rotate and the staff is consistently praised for steering guests toward smart pours. When your server can talk Cardinale vintages and guide you to a value Malbec in the same breath, you know the training is real.

bestValue.note: A genuine Argentine Malbec from one of Mendoza's benchmark producers at a steakhouse price that does not require a deep breath before ordering. Rich, dark-fruited, and built to stand up to a dry-aged ribeye.

hiddenGem.note: A Robert Parker 100-point Napa Bordeaux-style blend sitting on the list for those who know what they are looking at. If you are celebrating something real, this is the bottle.

skipThis.note: At $2,250 this is a flex bottle, not a value play. If you have the budget and the occasion, go with God. Everyone else should know that the Cardinale and the mid-tier Napa Cabs deliver serious experiences at a fraction of the price.

perfectPairing.note: The dry-aged funk and mineral char on that 18-ounce bone-in strip demand a red with grip and depth. The Catena Malbec brings dark fruit, leather, and just enough tannin to scrub the fat without overwhelming the beef. Classic steakhouse pairing done right.

bottomLine: Little Alley is the rare suburban steakhouse where the wine program matches the ambition of the kitchen. The call-ahead decanting service alone tells you everything — this is a restaurant that thinks about wine at the operational level, not just the inventory level. If you want to drink serious Cabernet with a serious steak without driving into Buckhead, this is your spot.

subtitle: Wine Spectator hardware, a sommelier forged at The Modern, and Italy from Friuli to Sicily
firstImpression: Osteria Mattone holds a Wine Spectator Award of Excellence every year since opening in 2013 and backs it up with roughly 180 selections and 1,500 bottles on site. Wine director Daniel Pernice trained at Danny Meyer's The Modern in New York during its James Beard Outstanding Wine Program run. He came home to Roswell and built the Italian wine list this city did not know it needed. If you care about Italian wine, this is a destination.
selectionDeepDive: The list covers Italy from Friuli to Sicily with the kind of depth that makes you forget you are OTP. Piedmont is properly represented with GD Vajra Albe Barolo anchoring the premium tier. Friuli shows up via Ronchi di Cialla Ribolla Gialla — a varietal most Roswell diners have never encountered. Tuscany delivers Chianti Classico Riserva and Antinori blends. Puglia, Abruzzo, Sardinia, and Campania all get their moments. Pernice champions lesser-known Italian regions with real conviction, and a Sommelier's Thoughts sidebar on the menu helps guests navigate unfamiliar territory. Smart Champagne and New World picks round out the edges without diluting the Italian core.
byTheGlass: Twenty-two wines by the glass priced from $13 to $25 span sparkling, white, rosé, and red with genuine range. The $25 GD Vajra Barolo by the glass is the crown jewel — you rarely see Barolo poured by the glass outside of major cities. Whites run from a $13 Centopassi Giato Bianco to a $17 Hess Panthera Chardonnay. The Aperitivo Happy Hour drops select glasses to $9 during the first two hours at the bar daily, making this one of the best wine happy hours in metro Atlanta.

bestValue.note: Puglia's answer to old-vine Zinfandel at a price that makes zero sense for the quality. Rich, ripe, and loaded with dark cherry. Order this with any red-sauce pasta and feel like you just outsmarted the menu.

hiddenGem.note: A $16 glass of Friulian white that most Americans cannot pronounce and fewer have tasted. Floral, textured, and built for the restaurant's seafood antipasti. This is Pernice showing you what Italian white wine can be beyond Pinot Grigio.

skipThis.note: A fine California Chardonnay but you did not come to Osteria Mattone to drink Russian River Valley. At $17 a glass you are in range of the Bramito della Sala from Umbria or the Sardinian Vermentino, both of which tell a better story at this table.

perfectPairing.note: Pernice calls Ripasso the bridge wine for American drinkers exploring Italian reds and he is right. The dried-grape richness and gentle tannin of the Ripasso meet the slow-simmered Bolognese with the kind of harmony that makes you stop talking and just eat. This is the pairing that converts people.

bottomLine: Osteria Mattone is not just the best Italian wine program in Roswell — it is one of the best suburban Italian wine programs in the Southeast. The Wine Spectator hardware is earned, the sommelier has a pedigree that would play in Manhattan, and the by-the-glass program lets you explore Italy one pour at a time without breaking the bank. Roswell's wine identity starts here.
halfPriceWineNight: Aperitivo Happy Hour daily at the bar — first two hours feature $9 select glasses

subtitle: Michelin Bib Gourmand fried chicken with a quietly smart wine list
firstImpression: Table & Main is famous for its Michelin Bib Gourmand fried chicken and its Simple, Seasonal, Southern soul, not for its wine list. But the backstory matters — the Pernice brothers who own this place also run Osteria Mattone's Wine Spectator-decorated cellar next door. Daniel Pernice, a sommelier trained at The Modern in New York, curates this list. It is deliberately compact and that is the point.
selectionDeepDive: Sixteen selections total, every single one available by the glass. The list leans New World with smart California, Oregon, and Washington picks alongside a handful of well-chosen French bottles. Willamette Valley Pinot Noir gets two entries — the Argyle Bloomhouse and Pick Road — which is a meaningful choice for a Southern restaurant. Washington's Hedges Red Mountain Syrah adds a smoky, savory option that plays beautifully with the pork chop. The quiet standout is a 2012 Borealis Vintners Riesling — a 14-year-old Pacific Northwest Riesling hiding on a fried chicken menu. That is not a distributor pick. That is a sommelier's wink.
byTheGlass: Every wine on the list is available by the glass, priced from $12 to $20. That is the whole program — no bottle-only holdouts, no upsell pressure. With most pours in the $12 to $15 range, you can work through fried chicken, shrimp and grits, and blackened catfish with a different glass for each course and still come in under budget. The Francois Montand Brut from Jura at $13.50 is a genuine celebration pour at a Bib Gourmand price.

bestValue.note: Willamette Valley Pinot Noir at thirteen dollars a glass in a Michelin-recognized restaurant. Light enough for fried chicken, structured enough for the pork chop, and priced like the operators want you to order a second round.

hiddenGem.note: A fourteen-year-old Pacific Northwest Riesling on a Southern comfort food menu is the kind of curveball that tells you a real wine person picked this list. The aged petrol notes and honeyed acid will absolutely sing with the fancy cornbread and honey butter.

skipThis.note: At $52 a bottle for a non-alcoholic sparkling option, the math is tough. If you are not drinking, save the money and enjoy sweet tea with your fried chicken — this is the South, after all.

perfectPairing.note: This sounds wrong but it works. The Syrah's smoky black pepper and dark fruit cut through the crispy buttermilk batter and the bird's natural richness. Red wine with fried chicken is an underrated move and the Hedges has enough acidity to keep it from getting heavy.

bottomLine: Table & Main proves that a small wine list can still be a smart wine list when the right person is choosing the bottles. You will come for the fried chicken and the Bib Gourmand credibility. The wine list will not be the main event, but it will quietly make your dinner better — and that is exactly what a reliable program should do.
halfPriceWineNight: T&M Mondays — 3-course prix fixe for $35 (wine pairs especially well at this price point)

subtitle: Sommelier-owned, 200 bottles deep, with an Enomatic machine doing the heavy lifting
firstImpression: Vin25 is tucked into a converted 1941 house on a quiet side street off Canton and it is exactly the kind of place you would walk past if nobody told you. Do not walk past. Owner Phillip Cooper holds a Court of Master Sommeliers certification and personally selects every bottle in a 200-plus wine cellar that spans France, Italy, Spain, Austria, and Hungary. An Enomatic wine preservation system means the by-the-glass program goes deeper than most restaurants would dare. This is wine as the main event.
selectionDeepDive: Two hundred bottles is substantial for any restaurant, let alone a cozy house in Roswell. Cooper curates across the major European regions — France and Italy anchor the list — but the real personality shows up in the margins. Austrian selections and Hungarian Tokaji tell you this is a sommelier shopping with conviction, not a manager ordering from a distributor sheet. Spain rounds out the Old World representation and domestic picks fill in the New World side. The list rotates with the seasonal menu, which changes roughly five times a year, so repeat visits reward curiosity.
byTheGlass: Twenty-five-plus wines by the glass powered by an Enomatic nitrogen preservation system. That machine is the secret weapon — it lets Cooper crack bottles he would never risk opening at a standard pour-and-pray wine bar. The result is a by-the-glass lineup that genuinely reflects the depth of the full bottle list rather than playing it safe with crowd-pleasers. If you are a by-the-glass explorer, this is your playground.

bestValue.wine: Ask the sommelier for the current value pick
bestValue.note: With five menu rotations per year, the value sweet spot shifts. Tell Cooper your budget and style preference and let the CMS-certified sommelier do his job. That is what you are paying for.

hiddenGem.note: Cooper specifically recommends pairing Tokaji with the cheese plate as a dessert course. Most Roswell diners have never tasted Tokaji and this is a genuinely special way to end a meal — golden, honeyed, and ancient in the best sense.

skipThis.wine: Playing it safe with familiar labels
skipThis.note: You did not drive to a sommelier-owned wine bar in a 1941 house to order the same Cabernet you buy at Kroger. Trust the list and trust the person who built it. That is the whole point of Vin25.

perfectPairing.note: The short rib with rosemary cream sauce is one of the kitchen's signatures and Cooper builds his by-the-glass program around these pairings. Ask for the current red recommendation from the Enomatic and let the food-wine synergy do its thing.

bottomLine: Vin25 is the Roswell wine bar that wine people have been quietly recommending to each other. A Court of Master Sommeliers-certified owner, a 200-bottle cellar with genuine range, an Enomatic machine keeping the by-the-glass program honest, and a fire-lit patio with live jazz on weekends. If you are building a Roswell wine crawl, this is a mandatory stop.

subtitle: A bourbon temple with a wine list that quietly holds its own
firstImpression: Walk into 1920 Tavern expecting a speakeasy cocktail bar with Pappy Van Winkle dreams and you are not wrong — 39 bourbons and a Smoked Old Fashioned with rosemary lead the charge. But scan past the bourbon wall and you will find a wine list that is more thoughtful than it has any right to be. Interesting Reds and Interesting Whites categories signal a buyer with curiosity, and the inclusion of South African Pinotage is the tell — the Aronowitz family brought their heritage from Cape Town to Canton Street.
selectionDeepDive: The wine list is organized by varietal with enough breadth to cover a globally influenced menu. Cabernet Sauvignon and Pinot Noir anchor the reds with multiple tiers running from $42 to $125 a bottle. The Interesting Reds section is where the personality lives — this is the buyer stepping outside the safe zone. Whites follow the same pattern with Chardonnay and Sauvignon Blanc as the anchors and an Interesting Whites category for the adventurous. The Pinotage is the headline — South Africa's signature grape rarely appears on Atlanta-area wine lists and its presence here is a direct nod to the owners' roots.
byTheGlass: By-the-glass pours run from $12 to $18 with solid varietal coverage across reds and whites. Nothing flashy, but the range means you can match wine to a menu that swings from Peri Peri Chicken to Lobster Spaghetti to Fried Green Tomatoes without feeling boxed in. The sweet spot is the $14 to $16 range where most of the interesting pours live.

bestValue.wine: Any $12 glass from the core lineup
bestValue.note: At twelve dollars a glass in a restaurant that positions itself as fine dining, you are getting a fair deal. The entry-level Malbec and Sauvignon Blanc both overdeliver at this price point.

hiddenGem.note: South Africa's signature grape on a Roswell wine list is the kind of detail that makes you look twice. The Aronowitz family is from Cape Town and this is their heritage in a glass — bold, earthy, and unlike anything else you will find on Canton Street.

skipThis.wine: The default Cabernet if you are not looking at the Interesting Reds
skipThis.note: The standard varietal lineup is solid but forgettable. The whole reason to drink wine at 1920 Tavern is the stuff in the Interesting Reds and Interesting Whites sections — that is where the buyer's personality comes through.

perfectPairing.note: South African wine meets South African-inspired chicken. The Pinotage has enough rusticity and dark fruit to handle the peri peri heat while the earthy undertones echo the roasted corn and red bean pilaf. This is a pairing the kitchen was born to serve.

bottomLine: 1920 Tavern is a bourbon bar that happens to have a wine list worth exploring. Monday Wine Nights, a globally influenced menu that creates real pairing opportunities, and a South African Pinotage hiding on a Canton Street wine list make this a wildcard worth playing. Come for the Smoked Old Fashioned, stay because the wine surprised you.
halfPriceWineNight: Monday Wine Night — weekly wine specials and featured pours
